title: practical english usage - Part 308
content:
- I found the car difficult to control at high speeds.

The police were checking everybody's papers, (     .. .controlling - The crowd was too big for the police to control. (= ... to keep in order.) means manage, direct, not check or inspect. Compare: Control is a ‘ false friend’  for people who  speak some languages. It generally 429 control optaTG...) not 'Short’ is the opposite of 'tali', and also of 'long', (     ... the contrary We use opposite (►  548), not contrary, to talk about contrasting words.

2 contrary         and   opposite not bitterly cold, (     On  the contrary, the night. ..) Daytime temperatures in the desert are very high. In contrast, the nights can be We   can emphasise a contrast with in contrast.

be wonderfully sweet and loving.

He did not make things easy for his parents. On  the other hand, he could often everything he could to annoy and worry them.

- lie did not make things easy for his parents. On  the contrary, he did not (     . .   he contrary, it was uW/  paid.} The job was boring but on the other hand it was well paid.

-   7 suppose the job was boring? * On  the contrary, it was really exciting.’ on the other hand, not on the contrary. Compare: suggested is not true, ɪf we  want to give the other side of a question, we  use On  the contrary is used to contradict - to say that what has been said or 1  on   the    contrary         and   on   the    other      hand 428 contrary She’s continually taking days off.

I can’t work with these continual interruptions.

Continual(ly) is preferred for things that happen repeatedly, often annoyingly.

I’ve been working almost continually/continuously since yesterday evening.

the last 48 hours.

There has been continual/continuous fighting on the border for actions, processes, etc.

Continual(ly) and continuous(ly) are both commonly used for uninterrupted 427 continual(ly)           and   contin      uo   us(ly)   _______ Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  430   country How dare you? Take your hands off me at once!

How dare you? is sometimes used as an indignant exclamation.

'Mummy,  can I draw a picture on the wall? ’ Don't you dare!’ unwanted things.

Don't you dare! is sometimes used to discourage people from doing 3 special     expressions She daren't tell him  what she thinks.

The  third person singular is also daren't, without -s.

I daren't look.

A  special negative form daren't (+ infinitive without to) exists in British English.

The old lady didn't dare (to) open the door.

She doesn't dare (to) go out at night.

an infinitive with or without to.

Dare is, however, quite often used in negative sentences. It can be followed by 2  negative       use;   daren't he thinks.) He's not afraid to say what he thinks. (More natural than He dares to say what generally use other expressions.

In modem  English, dare is not a very common verb. In an informal style, people 1  uncommon 431 dare ►    405 date     of   birth     and   birthday For information about countable and uncountable nouns, ►     119-120.

Would you rather live in the town or the country?

We live in the country just outside Manchester.

The  expression the country (the opposite of the town) is very common.

I like wild country best. (     I like a  wild country best.) Country (uncountable) = 'open land without many buildings’ .

2  uncountable         use How many countries are there in Europe?

Scotland is a cold country. Mexico is the country I know best.

Country (countable) =  'nation', 'land'.

1  countable        use 430 country        ______ vocabulary' •  433    different For the difference between different and other, ►    550.2.

The job's different than I expected, (   ... different from/to what I expected.) Before a clause, different than is also possible in British English.

American football is very different from/to soccer. (AmE .. . different from/ In American English, than is common.

From is generally used after different; many British people also use to.

different          from/to prepositions: She's very different from her sister.
